title: practical english usage - Part 320
content:
I've known her since Tuesday.

since + starting point It's been raining for weeks.

I've known her for three days, (     ... since three days.) for + period to the present. They  are not the same. Compare: For and since can both be used with a present perfect to talk about duration up 2  for    and   since      with    perfect      tenses:    the   difference I've had a headache all day.

We've been here (for) six weeks.

How long have you been waiting (for)?

And/or is not usually used before all.

We   can often leave out for in an informal style, especially with How long...?

How long have you been here for? (= Since when . ..?) How long are you here for? (= Until when ...?) A  present tense with for refers to duration into the future. Compare: not We've lived here for 20 years, (     We live here for 20 years.) not I've known her for a long time, (     Jknew-herfer a long-time.) not a present tense.

To  measure duration up to the present, we  use a present perfect tense (►  52.1), My boss will be in Italy for the next ten days.

We go away for three weeks every summer.

That house has been empty for six months.

I studied the guitar for three years at school for + period of time We   use for for duration - to say how  long something lasts.

469 for,    since,      in   and   from:     time Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary' •  470 forget and leave That was a really fun party.

In informal English, fun can also be used as an adjective before a noun.

Anne and Sam are a lot of Jun.

The party was fun, wasn't it? (     The party was Junny.) or people are enjoyable or entertaining.

Fun  is normally an uncountable noun. It can be used after be to say that things 471 fun    and   funny 469 ► from     and   since:    time at home.) not Oh  damn! I've left my umbrella at home, (     T't^Jbfgfmenmyumbrella However, we  normally use leave if we  mention the place.

Oh  damn! I've forgotten my umbrella.

We   can use forget to talk about accidentally leaving things behind.

470 forget       and   leave For since meaning ‘ as’  or ‘ because’ , ►    377.

For from ... to and from ... until, ►    613.       For more about tenses with since, ►    579.

From/Since the dawn of civilisation, people have made war.

From/Since the moment they were married, they've quarrelled.

She's been like that from her childhood, (   .. . since her childhood.) mean 'right from the start'.

From is sometimes possible with a present perfect, especially in expressions that not I'll be at home from Tuesday morning (on), (     .. .-sinee-Tuesday morning.) no ten. (   i  The sht>tru^s of^ensine&eighf-. ..) The shop was open from eight in the morning but the boss didn't arrive till From is used in other cases.

I had been working since six o'clock, and I was getting tired.

working fromsix-o’clock-...) not I've been working since six o'clock, and I'm getting tired, (     I've been starting point up to the present, or up to a past time that we  are talking about.

We use since (with a perfect tense) especially when we  measure duration from a I've been waiting since six o'clock. I've known her since January.

From his earliest childhood he loved music.

From now on, I'm going to go running every day.

I work from nine to five.

I'll be here from three o'clock onwards.

from/since + starting point things begin or began.

From and since give the starting points of actions, events or states: they say when 4 from      and   since vocabulary' •  472  get: basic structures and meanings Would you mind getting off my foot?

I often get up at five o'clock. I went to see him,  but he told me to get out.

refers to a movement of some kind. (For the difference between get Before an adverb particle (like up, away, out) or a preposition, get nearly always 3 get    + adverb      particle      or  preposition:       get    out For go + adjective {go green, go blind, etc), and the differences between get, go, become, turn, etc, ►    39-1.

We must get the house clean before your mother arrives.

It's time to get the kids ready for school. I can 't get my hands warm.
